speaker: interviewer
text: 1995, We're interviewing Murray Pantirer in Hillside, New Jersey.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my father, Lazar Pantirer. These pictures were taken in 1940 in Cracow. The Germans asked us to take pictures of the family. And what purpose, we didn't know exactly but, uh, that's where we take the pictures. We were hoping that, uh, we're going to be able to stay in Cracow. This is my mother, Bella Pantirer. When we were ordered to take the pictures, we were hoping that we will be able to make it in Cracow together and we don't have to be sent out as refugees. This picture's my older brother, Alte Hirsch Pantirer. He was 17 years old. It was taken in 1940. This is me, Mejzesz Pantirer. This picture was taken in 1940 in Cracow and I was 15 years old. This is my younger brother, Chaim Yosef Pantirer. Was taken in 1940 and he was 13 years old. This is the only pictures that I have, eh, from before the war. This is the list that either Oskar Schindler brought it himself, or he sent to somebody to Gross-Rosen in October of 1944 to take the people to be taken out from Gross-Rosen into Brunnlitz. And the name, Mejzesz, is that's what the Polish people who write down. In Polish, it would be [? Munjak, ?] but they being Jewish, they call him Mejzesz. And to this day, I have no idea who put me on that list. And also somebody said that my trade, the roof, was a sheet metal worker and I was not a sheet metal worker. And I'm thankful that somebody lied so I can be saved.

speaker: interviewee
text: Number 205. This picture was taken in Bindermichl, in the displaced person camp in 1946 in the fall, when we got engaged, Lucy and myself. To the left is Jack [? Oling, ?] Ivan Rosenbaum, myself, my beautiful wife, and [? Barry ?] [? Tyger, ?] a friend of mine. This picture was taken in 1949, my Aunt Sally Levenstein with a daughter that was born in 1948. As you know, they lost the two children in Auschwitz in '44. So they had a baby girl born November 1948, and my Uncle Isaac Levenstein.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? Rella ?] Levenstein. Now she's married. Her name is [? Rella ?] Fel-- Feldman. This is a document from Displaced Persons Commission. As I have told you before, I came under the quota of 200,000 displaced person but Harry S. Truman, then president, signed a special quota. When I got to America, I received the papers. They wanted to find out how I do in America. It's a good country, after all. This is in 1972, when Oskar Schindler came to visit, uh, the United States. We took him to South Plainfield, and we showed him, it's written down, Schindler Drive. We became builders and came to America in 1949, and we became builders in 1950. And since-- uh, whenever we start a development that needed new streets and new street sign names, we will always ask the township planning board to allow us at least one street to be named Schindler Place or Drive. And we have now in New Jersey over 20 streets in developments. And one thing I have to say, not one single township, council, or planning board refused us to name a street-- why we wished to name Schindler Drive or Schindler Court. And he himself was very pleased that in America, there's so many streets. I made a deal with my kids that if I loan them some money, they don't have to pay me no interest. But one thing they have to do on their new jobs is name at least one street Schindler Drive or Schindler Court. Here in West Orange, Normandie Estates, we-- just a few months ago in '95-- we dedicated a street, Schindler Terrace. On my left-hand side is my partner, Abraham Zuckerman, his son, Wayne Zuckerman, his son-in-law, Bernie [INAUDIBLE], myself. Then going back to the left-- my son-in-law, [? Allen ?] [? Pines, ?] my son, Larry Pantirer, and the son-in-law of Zuckerman, [? Stephen ?] [? Katz. ?] In 1972 on January 5, in Short Hills Catering, my wife and myself celebrated our 25th wedding anniversary, and with our honored guest, Oskar Schindler. And to my left-hand side, my Aunt Sally Levenstein, my uncle Isaac Levenstein, my wife, Oskar Schindler, myself, [? Mania ?] [? Mendlebaum ?] [? Simon ?] [? Mendlebaum, ?] Abraham Zuckerman, and [? Millie ?] Zuckerman. I told my friends then at the party who Oskar Schindler is. And they all put him on a chair and we danced with him. [INAUDIBLE], Murray Pantirer, and in the back is my brother-in-law [? Alex ?] [? Horowitz. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And this was at your party?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was at, uh, Short-- Short Hills Catering, same party, 1972. I want to make a remark. After the party, I took him into my house and he was in my house for two days. And he liked to drink. And I put down a bottle of French cognac, and he almost finished it at one seating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I saw his eyes flickering, I asked him, Oskar, what made you do it? Why did you help the Jewish people? And he said the story that he was a Nazi, he believed in a big Germany. But when he saw the brutality and the killing, he made up his mind that he, instead of working with the Germans, he will work against it, and he will save as many Jews that is possible. And I believed him, because, uh, he was very sincere, and he-- he did what he was-- what he told us in '72-- he did it in '42, '43, '44, and '45.

speaker: interviewee
text: My partner Abraham Zuckerman and myself. We commissioned an artist to make a bust of Oskar Schindler. He made four of them, one we keep in our office, one we gave to Steven Spielberg as a gift when he was honored in Israeli Bonds, and one we're going to give to the Holocaust Museum in New York, and one we're going to-- in the Holocaust-- in Kean-- Kean College Research Center. There's one thing I would like to say about Oskar Schindler. I would call him that he was a man with three Ws. He liked wealth, women, and whiskey. But when he turned around-- maybe that was the only way how he could save the Jewish people-- when he turned around, he made from the W, M. He was a mensch, he used the money, and he was marvelous. And he used the money not to just better himself, but to save the-- as I have said in the tape before-- to save a minimum of 1,200 Jews. Here in Jerusalem, in Israel, there's a Yad Vashem, the memory of the Holocaust. And there's an alley built that you plant a tree for the righteous Gentile. Oskar Schindler's tree was among the very first few trees that was planted in Yad Vashem as a righteous Gentile. Every time I come to Yad Vashem to Israel, I visited that tree. This is Mrs. Schindler. It was taken last year in 1994. This is the cemetery of Mount Zion, Catholic cemetery that Oskar Schindler is buried. We were taken there for the last two days of filming Oskar by Steven Spielberg. Every one of us laid a stone. And here is Mrs. Schindler, all the actors that played. We got all together to-- Stephen made for us a nice cocktail party in the King David Hotel. And I was here with all my family. This violin was played in Plaszow by Rosner when Amon Goeth and Schindler made parties for the other SS people and for the girls. Mr. Rosner was taken in 1944 in October to Gross-Rosen, where they took away his violin. He said then that this violin belongs to Schindler. Upon arriving in Brunnlitz, he told Schindler that they took away his violin. Oskar Schindler went to Gross-Rosen. And I don't know how, but somehow he got this violin, gave it back to him. And after the war, he was playing in the Hilton and the other places. And I found out that the-- the violin is available. So my partner Zuckerman and my self, and [? Rella ?] Feldman we got the violin from Mr. Rosner. And we dedicated this violin to the Holocaust Museum in Washington in honor of Stephen Spielberg for making the movie, This is the groundbreaking ceremony for the Holocaust Museum in Washington. Myself to the left, Zuckerman is holding the shovel, and our friend [? Sam ?] [INAUDIBLE], and a group of other dignitaries and some from the Congress that were to the groundbreaking ceremony. This is the opening of the Holocaust study in Kean College. Jerry-- myself [? Jerry ?] [? Friendsbaum, ?] President Weiss from Kean College, Ray [? Kushner, ?] and Sam [INAUDIBLE]. This is a letter that then Tom Kean, who was, uh, Governor of the state of New Jersey, had, uh, established an executive-- executive signing for Advisory Council of the Holocaust to educate in the public school. In [INAUDIBLE] Hebrew University, we dedicated, uh, a plaque for Oskar Schindler, and the money is used for a scholarship for underprivileged student. And every year, I get letters-- myself and my partner-- to whom the scholarship went. This is my proud family. I have a son and two daughters. Unless you understand my English, it's very hard for me to pronounce 'in-law.' So my daughter's husband is a son. And my son's, uh, wife is a daughter. And the same thing is my youngest daughter, Alicia, Alan is a son to me. So I have six children now and nine grandchildren. And may God watch over them and I hope that they will have good health. And above everything, they will be mensch. They will know the-- to help out the needy people and always stand out for the right.
